paper.title: RETRACTED: Ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia, non-specific colitis, and pervasive developmental disorder in children

claims.text: This paper has been retracted and its findings are not considered valid or reliable.

A cohort of children with developmental regression (pervasive developmental disorder) presented with ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia and non-specific colitis on endoscopic and histopathological examination.

loci.quote: Ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia, non-specific colitis, and pervasive developmental disorder in children

claims.text: The paper proposed a temporal and possible causal association between gastrointestinal disease (ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia/colitis), onset of developmental regression, and (as widely interpreted) MMR vaccination.

retrieved.title: RETRACTED: Ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia, non-specific colitis, and pervasive developmental disorder in children
retrieved.abstract: We investigated a consecutive series of children with chronic enterocolitis and regressive developmental disorder.12 children (mean age 6 years [range 3-10], 11 boys) were referred to a paediatric gastroenterology unit with a history of normal development followed by loss of acquired skills, including language, together with diarrhoea and abdominal pain. Children underwent gastroenterological, neurological, and developmental assessment and review of developmental records. Ileocolonoscopy and biopsy sampling, magnetic-resonance imaging (MRI), electroencephalography (EEG), and lumbar puncture were done under sedation. Barium follow-through radiography was done where possible. Biochemical, haematological, and immunological profiles were examined.Onset of behavioural symptoms was associated, by the parents, with measles, mumps, and rubella vaccination in eight of the 12 children, with measles infection in one child, and otitis media in another. All 12 children had intestinal abnormalities, ranging from lymphoid nodular hyperplasia to aphthoid ulceration. Histology showed patchy chronic inflammation in the colon in 11 children and reactive ileal lymphoid hyperplasia in seven, but no gra…
retrieved.rationale: This is the original Wakefield et al. 1998 paper published in The Lancet, which has been formally retracted. The claim directly addresses this work. The paper falsely linked MMR vaccination to autism and has been thoroughly discredited due to fraudulent data manipulation, undisclosed conflicts of interest, and ethical violations. It is the primary subject of the retraction.

retrieved.title: Retraction—Ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia, non-specific colitis, and pervasive developmental disorder in children
retrieved.rationale: This is the formal retraction notice from The Lancet (2010). It explicitly confirms that the original paper has been retracted and its findings are not considered valid or reliable, directly supporting the claim.

retrieved.title: A qualitative and quantitative analysis of open citations to retracted articles: the Wakefield 1998 et al.'s case.
retrieved.abstract: Abstract In this article, we show the results of a quantitative and qualitative analysis of open citations on a popular and highly cited retracted paper: “Ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia, non-specific colitis and pervasive developmental disorder in children” by Wakefield et al . , published in 1998. The main purpose of our study is to understand the behavior of the publications citing one retracted article and the characteristics of the citations the retracted article accumulated over time. Our analysis is based on a methodology which illustrates how we gathered the data, extracted the topics of the citing articles and visualized the results. The data and services used are all open and free to foster the reproducibility of the analysis. The outcomes concerned the analysis of the entities citing Wakefield et al . ’s article and their related in-text citations. We observed a constant increasing number of citations in the last 20 years, accompanied with a constant increment in the percentage of those acknowledging its retraction. Citing articles have started either discussing or dealing with the retraction of Wakefield et al . ’s article even before its full retraction happened in …
retrieved.rationale: A 2021 meta-analysis examining citations to the retracted Wakefield paper. It documents the retraction and analyzes how the scientific community has dealt with the retracted work. The paper acknowledges the retraction and provides systematic evidence of its status in the scientific literature.

retrieved.title: Ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia, non-specific colitis, and pervasive developmental disorder in children
retrieved.rationale: This appears to be a follow-up or related publication from Wakefield et al. (1998 listed but DOI from 2005), attempting to further the discredited vaccine-autism link. It contradicts the claim by attempting to reinforce findings that have been retracted.

retrieved.title: Detection of measles virus in children with ileo-colonic lymphoid nodular hyperplasia, enterocolitis and developmental disorder
retrieved.rationale: A 2002 paper claiming to detect measles virus in children with gastrointestinal and developmental disorders. This work attempts to support the discredited Wakefield hypothesis linking vaccines/measles to autism, contradicting the claim that those findings are invalid.

retrieved.title: MMR vaccination, ileal lymphoid nodular hyperplasia, and pervasive developmental disorder
retrieved.rationale: A 2002 publication examining the MMR vaccination-lymphoid hyperplasia-autism link. This appears to continue promoting the discredited connection, contradicting the validity of the retraction.

retrieved.title: The vaccine–autism connection: No link, still debate, and we are failing to learn the lessons
retrieved.rationale: A 2025 paper titled 'The vaccine-autism connection: No link, still debate, and we are failing to learn the lessons.' This appears to explicitly address and debunk the vaccine-autism connection promoted by the retracted Wakefield paper, supporting the claim that those findings are invalid.

retrieved.title: Ileal lymphoid nodular hyperplasia, non-specific colitis and regressive behavioural disorder: a new syndrome?
retrieved.rationale: A 1997 publication describing the syndrome presented in the Wakefield papers (ileal lymphoid hyperplasia with regressive behavioral disorder). This appears to be foundational work supporting the discredited link.

retrieved.title: Ileo-caecal lymphoid nodular hyperplasia non-specific ileo-colitis with regressive behavioural disorder and food intolerance: A case study.
retrieved.rationale: A 1997 case study of ileal-caecal lymphoid nodular hyperplasia with regressive behavioral disorder. This appears to support the syndrome later promoted by Wakefield, contradicting the claim of invalidity.

retrieved.title: Persistent messles virus (MV) infection of lleal lymphoid tissue in children with lise-colonic lymphoid nodular hyperplasia, entero-colitis and developmental disorder
retrieved.rationale: A 2001 paper claiming to demonstrate persistent measles virus infection in children with the syndrome. This appears to support the discredited Wakefield hypothesis linking measles/vaccines to developmental disorder.

retrieved.title: Persistent messles virus (MV) infection of lleal lymphoid tissue in children with lise-colonic lymphoid nodular hyperplasia, entero-colitis and developmental disorder
retrieved.rationale: Appears to be the same 2001 paper (duplicate entry with different DOI format) claiming persistent measles virus infection supporting the discredited vaccine-autism link.

retrieved.title: Ileal lymphoid nodular hyperplasia, non‐specific colitis and regressive behavioural disorder: a new syndrome?
retrieved.venue: Journal of Pediatric Gastroenterology and Nutrition
retrieved.rationale: A 1997 paper describing the syndrome of ileal lymphoid nodular hyperplasia with regressive behavioral disorder. This appears to be part of the foundational work supporting the later discredited Wakefield claims.

retrieved.title: Autism, Ileal-lymphoid-Nodular Hyperplasia and Measles
retrieved.rationale: A 2002 paper examining the link between autism, ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia, and measles. This appears to support the discredited vaccine-autism hypothesis.

retrieved.title: Ileo‐caecal lymphoid nodular hyperplasia non‐specific ileo‐colitis with regressive behavioural disorder and food intolerance: A case study.
retrieved.venue: Journal of Pediatric Gastroenterology and Nutrition
retrieved.rationale: A 1997 case study of ileo-caecal lymphoid nodular hyperplasia with regressive behavioral disorder and food intolerance. This appears to support the syndrome framework later exploited in the discredited Wakefield work.

topReasons.text: The Lancet published a formal retraction notice in 2010 explicitly retracting the paper, directly confirming the claim.
The original paper's record itself carries the 'RETRACTED:' prefix, documenting its status.
Independent 2021 scientometric analysis systematically documents the paper's retracted status and the field's acknowledgement of it.
A 2025 review reaffirms there is no vaccine–autism link, consistent with the findings being invalid.

verdicts.reasoning:
The claim is a DESCRIPTIVE meta-claim about the status of the paper itself: that Wakefield et al. (1998), 'Ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia, non-specific colitis, and pervasive developmental disorder in children,' has been retracted and its findings are not considered valid or reliable. This is a matter of documented historical record, not a scientific hypothesis whose empirical replication is uncertain.

Mechanism/strength: The claim requires only that (1) a formal retraction exists and (2) the scientific consensus rejects the findings. Both are firmly established. The retrieved literature includes the formal retraction notice from The Lancet (2010, 10.1016/s0140-6736(10)60175-4, 'Retraction, ...'), authored by The Editors of The Lancet, which directly confirms the retraction. The original paper's own title now carries the 'RETRACTED:' prefix in the record (10.1016/s0140-6736(97)11096-0). A 2021 scientometric analysis (Heibi & Peroni) independently documents the retracted status and the community's handling of it, and a 2025 review in Autism ('The vaccine–autism connection: No link, still debate') reaffirms there is no vaccine–autism link.

Weighing checks: All deterministic checks are 'na' (design elements not assessable, p-curve uninformative with k=0). These are neutral and irrelevant to a descriptive retraction claim; they neither support nor undermine it.

Corroboration summary: retracted=true is flagged explicitly. The 'contradicting_works' count (10) is a mischaracterization by the retrieval stance-labeler, those are the ORIGINAL Wakefield-aligned papers (companion 1997 abstracts, measles-virus detection papers by Uhlmann/Martin, etc.) that promoted the now-discredited hypothesis. They do NOT contradict the fact of retraction; they are precisely the discredited body of work the retraction concerns. Notably, the measles-virus-detection line (Uhlmann, O'Leary) was itself later shown to be non-reproducible and was central to the fraud findings. So these 'contradicting' items actually reinforce, rather than undermine, the claim about invalidity.

Base rate and disconfirming evidence: This is one of the most famous retractions in medical history, following the GMC's findings of dishonesty and the exposure of data manipulation (Brian Deer's investigation). Large-scale epidemiological studies (millions of children) found no MMR–autism link. There is no credible disconfirming evidence against the descriptive claim. The evidence is overwhelmingly one-sided and documentary.

Conclusion: Extremely high confidence. The only residual uncertainty is trivial (semantic scope of 'not considered valid or reliable'), warranting a small nonzero uncertainty rather than 1.0.

topReasons.text: The source paper was formally retracted by The Lancet in 2010, with GMC/investigations finding the clinical and histopathological findings were fraudulently misrepresented.
Follow-on works attempting to confirm the syndrome/measles-virus persistence share investigators and were themselves not independently corroborated.

verdicts.reasoning:
The claim is the central descriptive/clinical finding of Wakefield et al. 1998 (The Lancet): that a cohort of children with pervasive developmental disorder (developmental regression) presented with ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia and non-specific colitis on endoscopy/histology. Mechanistically this is a descriptive case-series observation, not a causal claim, but it is the empirical foundation on which the discredited MMR-autism hypothesis was built.

The decisive fact is that the source paper was FORMALLY RETRACTED by The Lancet in 2010 (evidence 10.1016/s0140-6736(10)60175-4). The retraction, together with the General Medical Council findings, established that the study was based on fraudulently manipulated and selectively reported data, undisclosed conflicts of interest, and serious ethical violations. The GMC found that the reported histopathological/clinical findings were misrepresented, the colonoscopy/biopsy records were altered to fit the narrative, meaning even the descriptive 'colitis'/'hyperplasia' findings are not trustworthy as reported. The core descriptive characterization was contradicted by the hospital's own pathology grading in the subsequent investigation.

Corroboration metrics: retracted=true, 1 failed replication, 10 contradicting works, and the 'supporting' works are the retraction notice and citation-analysis papers that themselves document invalidity. The high citedByCount (1993) reflects notoriety, not scientific support. The apparent 'supporting' follow-on works (Uhlmann, Martin) attempting to confirm measles virus persistence were themselves not independently replicated and are tainted by the same investigators.

Design checks are all 'na' (case series, no randomization/blinding/controls), consistent with a low-rigor uncontrolled case series with high risk of selection and reporting bias. Given formal retraction for data fraud specifically affecting the described clinical/histological findings, replication likelihood is very low. I retain modest uncertainty because non-specific bowel findings do occur in some autistic children, so a weakened version might partly hold, but the specific cohort finding as reported is not replicable.

topReasons.text: The paper was formally retracted by The Lancet in 2010 following findings of data fraud, undisclosed conflicts of interest, and ethical violations.
Design was an uncontrolled case series of only 12 children relying on parental recall for symptom-onset timing, incapable of establishing causal association and highly prone to recall/selection bias.

Large independent epidemiological studies and subsequent reviews consistently found no MMR–autism link; contradicting works far outnumber supporting works and no credible independent replication exists.

verdicts.reasoning:
The claim describes the central assertion of Wakefield et al. (1998) in The Lancet: a temporal and possible causal association between gastrointestinal disease (ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia/colitis), onset of developmental regression, and (as widely interpreted) MMR vaccination. Mechanism: the paper proposed that MMR vaccination triggers intestinal pathology which in turn causes or coincides with regressive developmental disorder (autism). This is a causal/temporal association claim based on a case series of only 12 children, with no control group, no randomization, and reliance on parental recall for the timing of symptom onset, an extremely weak design that at best establishes correlation, and one highly vulnerable to recall bias and selection bias.

The paper was formally RETRACTED by The Lancet in 2010 (evidence 10.1016/s0140-6736(10)60175-4). The retraction followed findings of fraudulent data manipulation, undisclosed conflicts of interest, and serious ethical violations. Wakefield was struck off the UK medical register. The corroboration record flags retracted=true, failed_replications=1, and contradicting_works=10. Large, well-conducted epidemiological studies (population-scale cohort and case-control studies across multiple countries) have consistently found NO association between MMR vaccination and autism, decisively failing to replicate the proposed link. The claimed measles-virus-detection follow-ups (Uhlmann, Martin) were not independently reproducible and used labs later shown to produce contamination artifacts.

The strongest disconfirming evidence is overwhelming and one-sided: formal retraction, documented fraud, weak uncontrolled design, and repeated large-scale failures to replicate. The 2025 review (10.1177/13623613251345281) reaffirms there is no vaccine–autism link. This is a textbook case of a discredited, non-replicable causal claim. Confidence in NON-replication is very high; I assign a very low replication likelihood.

meta.tokensNote: Paper cached across calls; Opus for adjudication.

narrative:
This paper is retracted. The retraction notice was published on 6 February 2010 (source: Retraction Watch), and its findings are not considered valid or reliable. That fact alone determines the verdict of unsupported, with an overall reproducibility likelihood of 3 percent (interval 0 to 8 percent). Anyone deciding whether to build on this work should stop at the retraction: the journal has formally withdrawn the paper, and the claim that it is retracted and invalid is itself the most robust element in the record at 92 percent.

The individual claims track this collapse. The central proposal of a temporal and possible causal association between gastrointestinal disease, onset of developmental regression, and (as widely interpreted) MMR vaccination is rated unsupported at 9 percent. Even the more limited descriptive claim, that a cohort of children with developmental regression presented with ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia and non-specific colitis on endoscopy and histopathology, is fragile at 18 percent, meaning the underlying observations themselves cannot be relied on. No transparency signals were recorded and no separate failing or warning checks were logged, because the retraction supersedes finer-grained assessment.

The wider literature confirms the picture rather than rescuing it. Of 14 related studies identified across 5 sources queried, 10 contradict the paper and 1 is a failed replication, against only 3 nominally supporting. The weight of independent evidence runs against the causal hypothesis. Do not trust or build on this paper.
